coredata.dc:title: A time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper for water induced information transmission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Zihan
Dong, Die
Yu, Shanshan
Xia, Siyuan
Duan, Yujie
Liu, Hui
Cheng, Fei
Wang, Lei
Zhu, Hongxiang
He, Hui
coredata.dc:description: The progressive presentation of multilevel information enhances the security level of information storage and transmission. Here, a time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper was developed by integrating cellulose nanofiber (CNF)-stabilized gold nanoclusters and CNF-modified long afterglow materials. The orange fluorescence of gold nanoclusters on nanopaper was regulated by the reversible swelling and shrinking of CNF induced by water solution, while the cyan fluorescence of micron-long afterglow remained stable and acted as the background signal. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence colour and intensity of the nanopaper could be freely adjusted between orange and cyan on the time scale. Therefore, the array information on the nanopaper could be encoded by a water solution, iterated variation as the step-by-step solvent volatilized on the time scale measured by the time of the afterglow duration. This work provides a new approach for constructing time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper for confidential information storage and transmission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofiber
Nanopaper
Water induced
Self-erasing
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and fluorescence properties of SAO-CNF
Responsive optical information encryption of TMSEP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22368009 , 22368010 , 22168008 , 22378085 ), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation ( 2021JJA120040 ), the Young Backbone innovative talent cultivation project of Guangxi University ( 2022BZRC014 ), the Innovative Development Special Academician Team Fund of Guangxi University ( 2022WSF0902 ), the Natural Science and Technology Innovation Development Doubling Plan of Guangxi University ( 2023BZJL026 ), the Technology Development Project of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd ( 202100039 , 202100041 ).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22368009, 22368010, 22168008, 22378085), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2021JJA120040), the Young Backbone innovative talent cultivation project of Guangxi University (2022BZRC014), the Innovative Development Special Academician Team Fund of Guangxi University (2022WSF0902), the Natural Science and Technology Innovation Development Doubling Plan of Guangxi University (2023BZJL026), the Technology Development Project of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co. Ltd (202100039, 202100041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation method of TMSEP and its application in message encryption.

(a) TEM image of AuNCs with insets depicting the particle size distribution and lattice size of AuNCs; (b) excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of AuNCs, as well as the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, with insets showing photographs of AuNCs under daylight (1) and under a UV lamp (365 nm, 2); (c) Au 4f XPS spectrum of AuNCs; (d) TEM image of CNF with an inset showing the diameter distribution of CNF; (e) C 1 s XPS spectrum of CNF; (f) O 1 s XPS spectrum of CNF; (g) TEM image of CNF-AuNCs; (h) EDS mapping image of CNF-AuNCs; (i) FTIR spectra of AuNCs and CNF-AuNCs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs water suspension; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CNF-AuNCs composite material water suspension, with an equivalent AuNCs content as the corresponding AuNCs water suspension; (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of AuNCs and CNF-AuNCs; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of CNF-AuNCs composite film; (e) CIE chromaticity diagram of CNF-AuNCs composite films with different AuNCs ratios (0 % refers to CNF film, while the others represent CNF-AuNCs composite films with 0.5 %, 1 %, 2 %, and 4 % AuNCs ratios) under excitation at 365 nm; (f) Photographs of CNF-AuNCs composite film under daylight (upper) and 365 nm UV irradiation (lower); SEM images of cross-sections of CNF-AuNCs composite film in (g) dry and (h) wet states with freeze-fracture preparation prior to SEM observation; (i) Schematic representation of the internal structure of CNF-AuNCs composite film in dry and wet states; (j) Water encryption and self-erasure process of CNF-AuNCs composite films.

(a) SEM image of SAO microstructure with EDS image; (b) SEM image of SAO-CNF microstructure with EDS image; (c) FTIR spectra of SAO, CNF, and SAO-CNF; (d) N 1 s XPS spectrum of SAO-CNF; (e) fluorescence spectra of SAO and SAO-CNF, inset showing photographs of SAO in daylight (1) and under UV light (365 nm, 2), and after turning off the UV light for 10 min (3); (f) X-ray diffraction patterns of SAO, CNF, and SAO-CNF; (g) digital photographs of SAO mixed with water and CNF dispersions and CNF-modified SAO in 0 h (top) and 12 h (bottom); (h) under UV light irradiation 10 min and then the UV lamp was turned off, the afterglow duration of SAO-CNF composite films with different ratios was varied.

(a) Emission fluorescence spectra of TMSEP solutions with different SAO contents; (b) CIE chromaticity diagrams (Ex = 365 nm) of the samples of TMSEP, SAO-CNF, and CNF-AuNCs solutions (1# refers to TMSEP solution containing SAO5%-CNF, 6# refers to TMSEP solution containing SAO50%-CNF), and the inset shows the CIE chromaticity diagrams of the three solutions under 365 nm UV light; (c) photographs of TMSEP under 365 nm UV light (top) and under UV light turned off after 10 min of irradiation (bottom); (d) encryption map of fluorescent solutions; (e) water encryption and self-erasure process of TMSEP.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of TMSEP immersed in solutions with different ethanol contents; (b) Corresponding relative fluorescence intensity over time (I0: initial fluorescence intensity, It: fluorescence intensity at t min); (c) Relative fluorescence intensity graphs of TMSEP over time for different initial ethanol concentrations; (d) Images of the self-erasing process of fluorescent patterns at different ethanol concentrations (80%, 40% and 0% in that order); (e) Photographs of encrypted information displayed by time scale.

head.ce:title: A time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper for water induced information transmission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, Nanning 530004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The progressive presentation of multilevel information enhances the security level of information storage and transmission. Here, a time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper was developed by integrating cellulose nanofiber (CNF)-stabilized gold nanoclusters and CNF-modified long afterglow materials. The orange fluorescence of gold nanoclusters on nanopaper was regulated by the reversible swelling and shrinking of CNF induced by water solution, while the cyan fluorescence of micron-long afterglow remained stable and acted as the background signal. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence colour and intensity of the nanopaper could be freely adjusted between orange and cyan on the time scale. Therefore, the array information on the nanopaper could be encoded by a water solution, iterated variation as the step-by-step solvent volatilized on the time scale measured by the time of the afterglow duration. This work provides a new approach for constructing time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper for confidential information storage and transmission.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The exponential progress of information technology has led to a heightened societal focus on the paramount issue of information security. In recent decades, a plethora of diverse information encryption materials have come to the fore, affording remarkable efficacy in securely storing and transmitting information, as well as making significant strides in the realms of information recording, anticounterfeiting, and privacy preservation[1–5]. Several highly effective examples encompass multidimensional encryption materials[6–9], fluorescent inks[10–13], dynamic fluorescent codes[14–17], and self-erasing materials[18–22]. Compared to other intricate encryption methods employed to enhance information security, self-erasing materials exhibit advantages such as ease of identification (reading), straightforward encoding, and convenient visualization[23]. Most captivatingly, these materials possess a distinctive temporal dependency characteristic, whereby the information stored within these materials has a limited lifespan. This feature not only enhances information security by thwarting data leakage after information retrieval but also serves as an upgrade to encryption systems, thereby further fortifying the confidentiality of the information.

The time-dependent fluorescent self-erasing material has emerged as an effective contender for the development of encryption materials, owing to its dynamic optical characteristics on a temporal scale and remarkable repeatability. This unique material enables information to be written, erased, cycled, and concealed, showcasing its potential for information encryption[24,25]. For example, Li and colleagues developed a reversible humidity-responsive fluorescence technique in which the fluorescence exhibits a transition between blue and yellow for transient fluorescence patterning and message encryption applications[26]. Wang's group developed a self-erasing rewritable paper in which the fluorescence transitions from present to absent, proving its suitability for transient message encryption[27]. In addition, Lie's group reported a rewritable light-emitting paper with fluorescence modulation characteristics responsive to humidity changes, showing a “write-erase-write” change in green fluorescence[28]. These studies provide a new perspective on the design of information encryption materials that can undergo reversible transformations in response to specific stimuli. However, in self-erasing materials, encrypted information often exhibits binary transitions (going from presence to absence), limiting the potential for complex information encoding[29,30]. Therefore, the development of self-erasing materials with higher security is encouraged. By interweaving different information from each time period and transmitting it on the same channel, extracting the information from each time period and reducing it to the original information at the receiving end, then combining it with the self-erasing effect, i.e., time-multiplexed self-erasing material, the information is presented progressively at multiple levels.

In essence, the anticounterfeiting approach based on self-erasing materials can be conceptually simplified as a combination of external stimuli (input) and fluorescence patterns (output). To achieve time-multiplexed effects, two common methods are typically employed. One approach involves presenting multilevel information changes on the time axis using different stimuli and requires a cumbersome decoding process. The other approach requires only a single stimulus, and information unfolds in multiple stages over time[31–33]. Luminescent materials with long afterglow characteristics can provide a multitude of fluorescence or even phosphorescence colours, introducing an additional time dimension that further elevates the complexity of information encryption[34–38]. The Sr4Al14O25: Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) phosphor is an important aluminate luminescent material with the advantages of high fluorescence intensity, long afterglow time and nonradioactive[39–42]. The integration of these two materials offers substantial support for building a more secure encryption system. Nevertheless, afterglow materials with low water dispersibility present challenges when merging with fluorescent materials in aqueous environments. Hence, there is a need to devise an assembly strategy that incorporates fluorescent and afterglow materials into self-erasing substances, enabling the stepwise presentation of multiple layers of information while maintaining stable intercomponent binding.

In this study a time-multiplexed self-erasing nanopaper (TMSEP) was developed by the assembly of cellulose nanofiber-stabilized gold nanoclusters (CNF-AuNCs) and CNF-modified long afterglow materials (SAO-CNF). The CNF-AuNCs and SAO-CNF within the TMSEP were used as solvent-responsive fluorescent switches and “message timers” (time indicators of when encrypted messages can exist), respectively. TMSEP exhibits solvent-induced reversible information recording, self-erasing, and time-limited decryption functionalities. This approach simplifies the encryption and decryption procedures and enhances information security through the automatic self-erasing mechanism facilitated by solvent-induced information encoding within the model, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . It is worth noting that all encrypted messages can only be decrypted in the presence of afterglow. The afterglow in TMSEP is adjustable in duration, so that the afterglow duration perfectly matches the presentation time of the encrypted message. Furthermore, the presentation time of different information can be controlled by regulating the solvent concentration. Within distinct time intervals, diverse pieces of information can be accessed, enabling the sequential presentation of multiple levels of information. It is also possible to hide correct information in erroneous data and mislead those who do not have “time keys”. This work provides a new approach to constructing self-erasing materials.

Bagasse pulp fibres (CFs) were obtained from Guangxi Guangye Sugar Co., Ltd. (Guigang, China). 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidoxy (TEMPO), sodium hypochlorite solution (NaClO), sodium chlorite (NaClO2), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), glutathione (GSH; for cell culture), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), dichloromethane (DCM), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, isopropylamine (IPA) and methanol (MeOH) were obtained from Aladdin. The NH2-Sr4Al14O25: Eu2+, Dy3+ (SAO) phosphors were obtained from Lu Ming Co., Ltd., Dalian, China. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water. The ambient environment and relative humidity were constant at 25 °C and 45 %, respectively.

First, 5.00 g of CFs was uniformly dispersed in 300 mL of distilled water to form a CF suspension. Subsequently, 0.06 g (0.4 mmol) of TEMPO, 0.30 g (2.0 mmol) of NaClO, and 4.52 g of NaClO2 were added to the CF suspension. Then, the suspension was stirred at 60 °C for 16 h, followed by the addition of 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol to terminate the reaction. The resulting product was washed with distilled water until the supernatant became neutral and diluted to 0.6 wt%. Finally, a uniform suspension containing carboxylated cellulose nanofibers was prepared through homogenization under high pressure and designated as CNF.

AuNCs were synthesized through chemical reduction. All glass containers were immersed in aqua regia for 20 min and rinsed three times with ultrapure water. A mixture containing 2.0 mL of HAuCl4 solution (20 mmol/L), 0.6 mL of GSH solution (100 mmol/L), and 17.4 mL of ultrapure water was added to a round-bottom flask and stirred at a low speed (500 rpm/min) for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was heated and stirred at 120 °C for 4 h. The solution was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain a powdered product. The product was named AuNCs and stored at 4 °C in the absence of light.

To fabricate CNF-AuNCs films with varying AuNCs contents, different volumes of AuNCs suspensions (1.5 mg/mL) were dispersed into a 0.5 wt% CNF suspension, followed by 30 min of stirring and ultrasonic defoaming. The resulting suspension was poured into polystyrene culture dishes and evaporated under static conditions at 25 °C for one week to prepare films with AuNCs/CNF ratios of 4 %, 2 %, 1 %, and 0.5 %, named CNF-AuNCsX (X = 4 %, 2 %, 1 % or 0.5 %). CNF film without AuNCs was prepared using the same procedure as the control sample.

The CNF suspension was magnetically stirred at room temperature. EDC and NHS with equal molar mass were added to the CNF suspension and stirred for 30 min. A certain mass of SAO was added to the suspension and stirred at room temperature for 8 h. Subsequently, the suspension was washed 4 times by centrifugation, poured into a polystyrene petri dish, and evaporated for one week at 25 °C under static conditions to obtain a dried membrane. Films with different SAO/CNF ratios (50 %, 40 %, 30 %, 20 %, 10 % and 5 %) were prepared and named SAOX-CNF films (X = 50 %, 40 %, 30 %, 20 %, 10 % or 5 %). As a control, CNF@SAO was prepared without modification and directly by comixing and stirring. The film named TMSEP was prepared by adding an equal volume of CNF-AuNCs4% to SAO-CNF.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument with the following configuration: Al Kα X-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV); a spot size of 400 μm, and an operating power of 75 W. The calibration of the binding energy scale was confirmed according to the recommended ISO standard for monochromatic Al Kα sources[43]. Full spectrum scans were performed with a pass energy of 100 eV and a step size of 1 eV; narrow scans were conducted with a pass energy of 50 eV and a step size of 0.1 eV. At least 5 cycles of signal accumulation were performed for narrow scans. A TESCAN MIRA LMS scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used for imaging sample morphology, energy spectrum mapping, and other tests. During morphology imaging, the acceleration voltage was set to 3 kV, whereas for energy spectrum mapping, it was adjusted to 15 kV. The SE2 secondary electron detector was employed during these processes. Using the American FEI Talos F200X G2 model (TEM), operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, high-resolution imaging of morphology was conducted, including point scanning of energy spectra, and area scanning of energy spectra, utilizing the EDS Super-X spectrum model. For TEM imaging of CNF, samples were negatively stained using a 2 % solution of phosphotungstic acid. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using the Agilent 8453 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) measurements were conducted on a SpectrumGX spectrometer. Samples were mixed with potassium bromide at a ratio of 1:100, ground into a powder, and then pressed into discs for testing. Photoluminescence (PL) absorption and emission spectra were recorded on an FS5 fluorescence spectrometer, with an excitation wavelength set to 365 nm. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using Multi-Channel Scanning (MCS) technology on the FS5 fluorescence spectrometer. Quantum yield was determined using quinine sulfate as a standard, excited at a wavelength of 365 nm. Delayed emission spectra were measured using a QuantaMaster 8000 spectrometer with the following settings: lamp frequency set at 50 Hz and initial delay of 1 ms. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer utilizing a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54056 Å) within a 2θ range of 5-80° with a step angle of 0.01°. Zeta potential measurements were carried out using the NANO ZS90 instrument; a 633 nm, 10 mW laser was employed, with water serving as the dispersant. Zeta potential values were calculated using the Smoluchowski model with a dielectric constant of 78.5 and a reflective index of 1.33, and each sample was tested repeatedly to obtain an average. The relevant parameters of the samples are depicted in the legend. Fluorescence and phosphorescence images were acquired with 365 nm excitation in a darkroom UV analyser (ZF-20D) using a smartphone as a signal collector.

To prepare self-erasing papers related to information encryption, we prepared TMSEP by utilizing CNF as a backbone and introducing fluorescent and afterglow materials (AuNCs and SAO). AuNCs were synthesized by a chemical reduction method. The morphology of the AuNCs was characterized by TEM, which suggested that the AuNCs could be uniformly dispersed in solution and had a spherical structure (Fig. 2 a). Meanwhile, the particle size of AuNCs was mainly distributed between 1.4 and 2.8 nm, and the lattice spacing was 0.235 nm, corresponding to the Au (1 1 1) plane of AuNCs[44]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs exhibited excitation and emission peaks at 410 nm and 610 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b), and AuNCs appeared pale yellow under ambient daylight conditions (Fig. 2b, No. 1) and demonstrated orange–yellow fluorescence (Fig. 2b, No. 2) under UV light (365 nm). Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 410 nm was exhibited in the UV–visible absorption spectra of AuNCs, corresponding to the position of the excitation peak (Fig. 2b). As shown in Fig. 2c, two components corresponding to Au(I) and Au(0) appeared in the Au 4f7/2 XPS spectrum of AuNCs, with binding energies of 84.3 eV and 83.7 eV, respectively, and the Au(I) content percentage was 75 %[45,46]. In addition, the S 2p XPS spectrum of AuNCs showed the presence of an Au-S bond with a binding energy of 162.5 eV (Fig. S1). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S2, the disappearance of the SH stretching band at 2524 cm−1 on the surface of GSH-capped AuNCs suggested that the SH bonds of GSH were broken, followed by the formation of stable Au-S bonds[47]. These results indicated that AuNCs were successfully prepared.

CNFs were prepared according to a previously reported research method. TEM was used to characterize the morphology and size of CNF (Fig. 2d) and CNF width in the range of 3 ∼ 9 nm, which was of great significance for its dispersion in water and self-assembly with AuNCs[48]. The XPS spectrum of CNF is shown in Fig. 2e. The C 1 s XPS spectrum of CNF revealed four peaks at 284.39 eV (14.5 %, CC/CH), 286.02 eV (66.8 %, COC/COH), 287.43 eV (14.3 %, β-glycosidic bonds), and 288.21 eV (4.4 %, O-CO)[49]. The O 1 s XPS spectrum of CNF (Fig. 2f) demonstrated the presence of abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CNF, which could be conducive to its assembly with AuNCs by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. S3, the zeta potentials of CNF and AuNCs were -36 mV and 5 mV, respectively, while the assembled CNF-AuNCs exhibited a neutralized negative charge characteristic (-20 mV), which indicated the existence of electrostatic attraction between CNF and AuNCs. Thus, AuNCs were stabilized on the CNF network structure (Fig. 2g and Fig. S4). The elemental mapping images revealed a uniform distribution of Au, N, and S in the CNF-AuNCs and formed a web image that highly coincided with the HAADF image, further substantiating that AuNCs were successfully stabilized on the CNF (Fig. 2h, Fig. S5 and Table S1). The successful stabilization of AuNCs could also be demonstrated by the presence of amide bands in the FTIR spectrum of CNF-AuNCs (Fig. 2i).

The fluorescence properties of aqueous suspensions of pure AuNCs and aqueous suspensions of CNF-AuNCs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, 3b and Fig. S6, it was evident that the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm increased with increasing AuNCs content, indicating a pronounced concentration dependence of AuNCs and confirming that the fluorescence intensity of the CNF-AuNCs in the aqueous suspension was slightly higher than that of pure AuNCs at the same concentration, indicating that the addition of CNF was beneficial for enhancing the fluorescence property of AuNCs.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S7a and Fig. S7b, the quantum yield (QY) values for AuNCs and CNF-AuNCs were 1.01 % and 1.31 %, respectively, and the QY of CNF-AuNCs was improved by nearly 30 % compared to that of AuNCs. Meanwhile, the incorporation of CNF increased the fluorescence lifetime of AuNCs from 1.727 μs to 2.132 μs (Fig. 3c, Fig. S7c, d and Table S2). These enhancements were mainly attributed to the stabilization effect of CNF, which immobilized the AuNCs on CNF through electrostatic adsorption as well as hydrogen bonding, limited the molecular motion of AuNCs, and thus suppressed the nonradiative transition. In addition, the water suspension of CNF-AuNCs exhibited good dispersion and maintained high fluorescence intensity even after being stored at 4 ℃ for 5 months (Fig. S8), which can be attributed to the electrostatic, hydrogen bonding, and physical barrier interactions between CNF and AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 3d, 3e, and 3f, compared with the corresponding CNF-AuNCs (water suspension), the fluorescence intensity of the CNF-AuNCs dry film was stronger and increased with AuNCs content, which was consistent with the fluorescence behaviour in the solution state.

To more thoroughly elucidate the fluorescence enhancement mechanism, the fluorescence properties of CNF-AuNCs before and after drying were investigated in this study. Compared to the CNF-AuNCs suspension (2.132 μs), CNF-AuNCs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties after drying, extending their fluorescence lifetime to 13.085 μs (Fig. S9a and Table S2). Moreover, relative to the QY of CNF-AuNCs in aqueous solution (1.31 %), the QY of solid-phase CNF-AuNCs was significantly improved to 19.58 % (Fig. S9b). The large increase in the fluorescence lifetime and QY of dried CNF-AuNCs could be attributed to the tendency of CNF to entangle and aggregate during drying, resulting in a large restriction of CNF on the vibration, rotation, and torsion of AuNCs molecules in solids. In other words, AuNCs were tightly held in the microenvironment through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. The vibration and rotation of AuNCs were significantly suppressed, and the physical constraints hindered nonradiative pathways while activating radiative decay, leading to the observed enhancement in fluorescence emission in the experiment. As a result, a higher QY and longer fluorescence lifetimes were observed in the dried state with improved emission intensity.

Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the CNF-AuNCs films upon immersion in different solvents was investigated, including dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, isopropanol (IPA), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), and ethanol (EtOH) (Fig. S10a). Compared with the fluorescence intensity in the dry film state, the fluorescence intensity of all films immersed in solvent decreased, and the fluorescence intensity of films immersed in water decreased most obviously. As shown in Fig. S10b, the emission spectral intensity of the CNF-AuNCs film immersed in different percentages of ethanol varied, with a decrease in fluorescence intensity as the water content increased. CNF-AuNCs films encapsulating AuNCs can be selectively activated and controlled by water due to the specific response of CNF films to water (Fig. 3g–i). As shown in Fig. 3j, by exploiting the fluorescence chromatic difference between the emission and quenching states of AuNCs, diverse patterns, such as butterflies and roses, could be created. It is worth noting that the patterns remained intact for a duration of 3 min, and after 11 min, all patterns were completely erased, resulting in a blank fluorescent film. That is, the information spontaneously self-erased over time, resulting in a blank fluorescent film because the CNF-based film underwent fibre consolidation after the water was continuously evaporated, leading to tighter interweaving between individual fibres and compacting the layers. Consequently, the dispersed AuNCs become tightly restabilized to the CNF substrate, restricting their molecular motion and reducing nonradiative energy dissipation, thus facilitating fluorescence restoration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and fluorescence properties of SAO-CNF
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of CNF-modified SAO before and after are shown in Fig. 4 . Compared with the surface of pure SAO (Fig. 4a and Fig. S11), the surface of SAO-CNF was highly altered (Fig. 4b), which could be attributed to the encapsulation effects of CNF on SAO. As shown in Fig. 4a and 4b, characteristic elements such as Al and Sr were uniformly distributed on the SAO surface. In contrast, the surface elements of SAO-CNF were unevenly distributed. The EDS elemental analysis showed that the characteristic elements (Al, Sr, Eu, Dy) on the modified SAO surface were significantly reduced (Fig. S12, Fig. S13, Table S3 and Table S4), which was due to the modified behaviour of CNF, leading to the formation of a cellulose layer on the SAO surface. However, further analysis is needed to confirm the mode of CNF attachment on the SAO phosphor surface. Therefore, the FTIR spectra of CNF, SAO, and SAO-CNF are shown in Fig. 4c. Compared to CNF, the FTIR spectrum of SAO-CNF exhibited new characteristic peaks at 746 cm−1, 585 cm−1, and 541 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration modes of Sr4Al14O25 in SAO[50]. In comparison to SAO, there was a significant enhancement in the absorption band near 3420 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. Strong absorption was also observed at 1080 cm−1, which was mainly attributed to the stretching vibrations of COC. Moreover, the vibrations at 1635 cm−1 and 1475 cm−1 could be assigned to the formation of amide bonds, indicating successful modification of SAO. In addition, XPS analysis was performed to further confirm the surface elemental composition and chemical bonding states of the SAO-CNF, as shown in Fig. 3d. The N 1 s XPS spectrum of SAO-CNF had two peaks at 399.50 eV and 401.63 eV, which were attributed to CN (61.2 %) and OC-N (38.8 %), respectively. The results further indicated that CNF was grafted onto SAO by an amination reaction.

Next, the fluorescence spectrum of SAO-CNF was studied. As shown in Fig. 4e, the excitation spectrum of SAO-CNF was similar to that of CNF-AuNCs, while the emission wavelength was significantly different, which provided a prerequisite for double emission. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission spectra of the modified SAO-CNF were consistent with those of SAO, indicating that the modification of CNF does not affect its fluorescence emission. The substantial spectral overlap between their transient and delayed convergence spectra suggests that the luminescence of SAO and SAO-CNF was consistent with the observable long afterglow (Fig. S14a, b). Moreover, SAO appears as a white powder in daylight (Fig. 4e, No. 1); under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, it emitted intense blue fluorescence (Fig. 4e, No. 2). After removing the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, a persistent blue afterglow appeared (Fig. 4e, No. 3), which was attributed to the 4f65d1 to 4f7 transition of the typical Eu2+ luminescent centres in SAO.

Since the crystal structure of SAO-CNF is closely related to its fluorescence properties, XRD was used to characterize the crystal structures of SAO and SAO-CNF (Fig. 4f). Compared with SAO (JCPDS 74–1810) with crystal plane indices of (200), (411), (021), (421), (801) and (820)[50], the XRD pattern of SAO-CNF had a broad band at 2θ = 13-25°, which could be attributed to CNF. This indicates that the crystal structure of SAO was preserved, thus ensuring the luminescent properties of SAO-CNF. In addition, the homogeneous dispersion of SAO in aqueous solution is the key to the preparation of SAO-CNF. As shown in Fig. 4g and Fig. S15, SAO is insoluble and not dispersed in water, and easily forms a large amount of settlement at the bottom in water. CNF with a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups grafted on SAO changed the hydrophobicity of SAO and ensured good aqueous dispersibility. The -21.5 mv zeta potential of SAO-CNF confirmed good aqueous dispersibility (Fig. S16). Meanwhile, SAO-CNF did not settle after 12 h, and the fluorescent properties were still well maintained in water. However, the aqueous dispersibility of SAO@CNF prepared by the physical mixing method was far from that of SAO-CNF, and the SAO in SAO@CNF settled after 12 h, resulting in a small amount of precipitation at the bottom. The results indicated that the modified SAO-CNF has good aqueous dispersion.

SAO is an inorganic pigment with a quartz-like structure. When the SAO content in the fluorescent film is too low, the role of luminescent centres becomes less prominent, and the trap depth and density of SAO may also change, leading to variations in the fluorescence brightness level of the fluorescent material. Therefore, to investigate the effects of different SAO additions on the luminescence properties of SAO-CNF solid films, we prepared SAO-CNF solid films with various SAO contents for study. As shown in Fig. 4h, with increasing SAO content, the luminous centre of SAO-CNF under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation became brighter, and its afterglow lasted longer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responsive optical information encryption of TMSEP
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectra of TMSEP solutions containing different amounts of SAOX-CNF are shown in Fig. 5 a. The fluorescence intensity of TMSEP solutions at Em = 490 nm increased with increasing SAO content, indicating that the TMSEP fluorescence solution was obviously concentration-dependent on SAO. Moreover, Fig. 5b shows the CIE chromatograms of SAO-CNF, TMSEP and CNF-AuNCs solution samples under 365 nm excitation. The emission colours of the samples labelled 1# ∼ 6# were significantly blueshifted from orange (0.404, 0.379) to cyan (0.251, 0.388) as the SAO content increased. When the fluorescence properties of TMSEP were investigated, it was found that it was similar to that of the TMSEP solution. Thus, under ultraviolet light irradiation, the fluorescence colour of TMSEP changed from orange to cyan when the SAO content changed from low to high (Fig. 5c).

Due to the unique fluorescent and phosphorescent properties of the TMSEP solution, it has significant anticounterfeiting potential. As a conceptual demonstration, transparent CNF, CNF-AuNCs, SAO-CNF, and TMSEP solutions were used as inks (Fig. 5d) to write different strokes of the Chinese character “王” (Wang) on black paper. The Chinese character “王” (wang) was visible under visible light, while the character “土” (earth) was visible under 365 nm UV light. After 10 min of exposure to 365 nm UV light, the UV light was turned off, and the character “二”(two) began to appear. Different digital encryptions also occurred when the solution filled the digital mould. Under UV light, the digits appeared as “8888″. However, when the UV light was extinguished, the number changed to ”1212″. Interestingly, we observed that TMSEP, similar to the CNF-AuNCs composite film, also possessed the potential for information encryption. As shown in Fig. 5e, a mask was used to spray water on TMSEP containing SAO30%-CNF to form a rose pattern. Over time, the information gradually self-erased and became almost invisible after 11 min. Simultaneously, after turning off the UV light, it was found that the afterglow of TMSEP also disappeared after 11 min.

In the experiment, TMSEP exhibited dual emission at 490 nm and 610 nm (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S14c), which belonged to SAO-CNF and CNF-AuNCs, respectively. By controlling the water-to-ethanol ratio for immersing TMSEP, a concentration-dependent emission peak at 610 nm was observed, and the fluorescence intensity decreased more significantly with decreasing ethanol concentration (Fig. 6a), which was consistent with the properties of AuNCs; the only difference was that the SAO-CNF module within TMSEP remained unaffected by the solvent and maintained a constant fluorescence intensity, which did not affect the self-erasable property of TMSEP (Fig. S17).

Inspired by the self-erasing process guided by the physical evaporation of liquids, we proposed an interesting hypothesis in which the fluorescent properties of the material can be controlled by different liquids over time, thereby further advancing the development of “time-multiplexed” information encryption and storage materials. In fact, the remaining time of cyan fluorescence is primarily determined by the evaporation rate of the solution, which in turn is influenced by the ethanol concentration. To further investigate the influence of these factors, we employed solutions with varying ethanol contents to study the fluorescence intensity variation during the self-erasing process of TMSEP over time. The fluorescence intensity ratio-time relationship graph further elucidated the variation in the fluorescence intensity of TMSEP over time (Fig. 6b). The decrease in ethanol content resulted in a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity and prolonged the remaining time. For instance, the remaining time could be controlled to shift from a long duration (11 min) with weak orange fluorescence (17 %) to a short duration (3 min) with brighter fluorescence (39 %). By controlling this parameter, TMSEP was successfully developed, with the response time being adjustable between 3 min and 11 min (Fig. 6b, 6c and Fig. S18). Exploiting this feature, advanced information storage and encryption materials with time-dependent security were further developed. The information, in the form of a “star-shaped” pattern, was encoded in the erasable paper using a brush dipped in a mixed solution of water and ethanol. Over time, the information spontaneously self-erased, resulting in a blank erasable paper. By controlling the ethanol concentration, the lifetime of the information was adjusted to 3, 6, and 11 min (Fig. 6d).

These results encourage further development of time-multiplexed information storage and encryption materials, where information not only undergoes self-erasing over time but also presents multilevel information progressively along the time axis, potentially accompanied by false information. Different pieces of information are interwoven over different time periods, and only a specific “time key” can be used to decrypt the information (Fig. 6d). For example, the information “GXU” was encoded using solutions with different ethanol concentrations, which allowed us to distinguish “GXU,” “GX,” and “Blank” (empty content) at distinct time intervals of 0, 3, and 11 min, respectively. The results indicated that the accurate information “GX” was only displayed during the 3 ∼ 11 min time interval; if read at incorrect times, erroneous information (GXU) or blank information would be obtained. For instance, we set up an encrypted message (884615) by encoding the message “88″ with three different ethanol concentration solutions. During the self-erasing process, the remaining encryption information (46 and 15) appeared in turn. Combining all the information could be used to display the encrypted numerical information ”884615″. When the information is completely erased, another set of information can be re-encoded. By analogy, the encrypted messages (“903711″ and ”890471″) can be encrypted step by step, and the final password can be decrypted only if the information of each time period is obtained (Fig. S19). In addition, it was possible to hide true information among falsehoods. In addition, this method also encoded information with opposite meanings, such as “believe” and “lie”, suggesting that an individual without the correct “time key” would recognize the contradictory information so that the security of the information is effectively guaranteed. What is interesting is that every encrypted message has a lifespan of just 11 min. Beyond this timeframe, all messages become unreadable. Furthermore, the afterglow period was ingeniously set at 11 min, meaning that the information can only be decrypted when the afterglow is still active. Notably, by recycling TMSEP, it was found that it could be reused more than 20 times with stable changes in fluorescence intensity, indicating that it has excellent reusability (Fig. 6d, Fig. S20 and Fig. S21).

In summary, a time-multiplexed message encryption scheme based on fluorescent self-erasing paper was developed. TMSEP was prepared from CNF-stabilized AuNCs and CNF-modified SAO. The orange fluorescence of AuNCs on TMSEP was regulated by the reversible swelling and shrinking of CNF induced by water solution, while the cyan fluorescence of the micron-long afterglow remained stable and acted as the background signal. Thus, TMSEP could be treated with solvents of different water contents to encode encrypted information. As the solvent evaporated, the encrypted information exhibited iterative changes, which allowed different data to be retrieved at different time intervals, resulting in a hierarchical progression of information encryption. The duration of the afterglow was used to measure the timeliness of the information. This work not only demonstrates the assembly process among CNF, fluorescent and afterglow materials but also highlights its unique advantages in the context of time-correlated security encryption. We aspire for this study to provide guidance in the rational design of new luminescent materials with time-dependent security features and posit them as ideal candidates for applications in the domain of information encryption.

Zihan Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Die Dong: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Shanshan Yu: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Siyuan Xia: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yujie Duan: Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hui Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Fei Cheng: Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lei Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Hongxiang Zhu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hui He: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.12.140.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
